learning_objectives: Identify unique characteristics of IT projects that affect management approach
Categorize IT project types: development, infrastructure, integration, migration
Understand IT project failure rates and root causes
readings: Schwalbe, Information Technology Project Management, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: IT projects fail at higher rates than other industries. Understanding why is the first step to managing them successfully.
key_concepts: IT project failure statistics: CHAOS report shows only ~30% fully successful
Common failure causes: unclear requirements, scope creep, poor stakeholder engagement, technical debt
IT project types: greenfield development, system integration, data migration, infrastructure deployment, SaaS implementation
Technical complexity vs. organizational complexity
DevOps and CI/CD impact on IT project management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a failed IT project case study (NHS National Programme for IT) and identifying root causes

review.value: IT requirements change faster than in construction or manufacturing; adaptive approaches are often essential
Shortcuts taken to meet deadlines create future rework costs; must be managed as a risk

learning_objectives: Elicit and document IT requirements effectively
Evaluate build vs. buy vs. SaaS decisions
readings: Bass, Software Architecture in Practice, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Requirements are the foundation. Getting them wrong is the most expensive mistake in IT projects.
key_concepts: Requirements types: functional, non-functional (performance, security, scalability), constraints
Elicitation techniques: interviews, workshops, prototyping, observation, document analysis
Build vs. buy vs. SaaS: cost, customization, time-to-market, dependency trade-offs
Architecture decision records: documenting technical decisions with rationale
Proof of concept: validating technical feasibility before full commitment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating a build vs. SaaS decision for a customer relationship management system

review.value: Often neglected but cause the most expensive rework; define performance, security, and scalability requirements early
Small, time-boxed experiment to validate a technical approach before committing to it in the project plan

class_schedule.topic: IT Project Estimation and Release Planning
learning_objectives: Apply IT-specific estimation techniques including function points and story points
Plan release strategies: big bang, phased, parallel, pilot
Manage technical dependencies in release planning

lecture_notes.introduction: IT estimation is notoriously difficult because of the intangible nature of software. Using multiple techniques and historical data improves accuracy.
key_concepts: Function point analysis: measuring software size by business functionality
Story points: relative sizing for agile estimation
Wideband Delphi: expert consensus estimation
Release strategies: big bang (all at once), phased (incremental), parallel (old and new simultaneously), pilot (limited audience)
Feature flags: deploying code without activating it, enabling gradual rollout
lecture_notes.demonstration: Estimating a web application using both function points and story points, then comparing results

review.value: Measure software from the user's perspective: inputs, outputs, queries, files, interfaces; technology-independent
Phased releases reduce risk vs. big bang but extend the transition period; pilot reduces blast radius of failures

class_schedule.topic: IT Quality Assurance, Testing, and Security
learning_objectives: Design a comprehensive testing strategy for IT projects
Integrate security into the project lifecycle (shift-left)
Manage defects and technical debt as project risks

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality in IT means the system works correctly, performs well, and is secure. Testing must be planned as a first-class project activity.
key_concepts: Testing levels: unit, integration, system, user acceptance testing (UAT)
Test automation: CI/CD pipeline integration, regression test suites
Security testing: SAST (static), DAST (dynamic), penetration testing
Shift-left: moving quality and security activities earlier in the lifecycle
Defect management: severity, priority, triage, fix-or-defer decisions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a test strategy for a banking application with security and performance requirements

review.value: Finding defects during design costs 10x less than finding them in production; invest in early testing and code review
User Acceptance Testing verifies the system meets business needs; must be done by actual users, not the development team

class_schedule.topic: IT Governance, Change Management, and Operations Handoff
learning_objectives: Apply ITIL service management concepts to project transitions
Plan operational readiness and support model

lecture_notes.introduction: An IT project is not done when the code is deployed. It is done when operations can support it and users are productive.
key_concepts: ITIL service transition: plan, build, test, deploy, review
Operational readiness: monitoring, alerting, runbooks, support team training
Change management (ITIL): change advisory board, change types (standard, normal, emergency)
Support model: L1/L2/L3 support tiers, SLAs, escalation procedures
Hypercare period: elevated support after go-live to catch and fix issues quickly
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating an operational readiness checklist and support model for a SaaS application deployment

review.value: Typically 2-4 weeks of elevated support after go-live; project team remains available to fix issues before full handoff
Step-by-step operational procedures for common tasks and incident response; must be written and tested before go-live

class_schedule.topic: Cloud, Data, and Emerging Technology Projects
learning_objectives: Manage cloud migration and SaaS implementation projects
Address data management and governance in IT projects
Assess emerging technology risks: AI, IoT, blockchain

lecture_notes.introduction: Technology evolves rapidly. IT project managers must stay current on technology trends that affect project planning and risk.
key_concepts: Cloud migration strategies: rehost, replatform, refactor, replace, retire, retain
SaaS implementation: configuration vs. customization trade-offs
Data migration: extraction, transformation, loading, validation, reconciliation
AI/ML project challenges: data quality, model validation, bias, explainability
Emerging technology risk assessment: maturity, vendor stability, skills availability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a cloud migration strategy for a legacy application portfolio using the 6 R's framework

review.value: Rehost (lift and shift), Replatform (lift and optimize), Refactor (re-architect), Replace (SaaS), Retire (decommission), Retain (keep as-is)
Data migration is often underestimated; plan for data cleansing, mapping, validation, and rollback

questions.question: A company must choose between building a custom CRM (estimated $800K, 18 months, full customization) and implementing Salesforce SaaS ($300K/year subscription, 6 months implementation, limited customization). The company has unique sales processes. Analyze the trade-offs and recommend an approach.
questions.answer: Build custom: higher upfront cost ($800K) but no recurring fees, full customization for unique processes, full control over data and roadmap, but longer time-to-market and ongoing maintenance cost. Salesforce SaaS: lower initial cost but $300K/year ongoing (break-even at ~2.7 years), faster deployment (6 months vs. 18), limited customization may require process changes, vendor dependency for roadmap and availability. Recommendation: Start with Salesforce if the unique processes can be adapted to fit 80% of Salesforce's standard workflow, because the faster time-to-value and lower risk justify the ongoing cost. If the unique processes are a competitive advantage that cannot be compromised, build custom. A proof of concept with Salesforce would resolve this uncertainty.

questions.question: Why are non-functional requirements often the cause of IT project failures, and give two specific examples of non-functional requirements that are commonly overlooked.
questions.answer: Non-functional requirements are often overlooked because stakeholders focus on 'what the system does' (features) rather than 'how well it does it' (qualities). They are also harder to elicit because users assume the system will be fast, secure, and reliable without stating it explicitly. When discovered late, they require architectural changes that are far more expensive than feature changes. Two commonly overlooked examples: (1) Scalability: a system designed for 100 concurrent users fails when 10,000 users access it after a marketing campaign; fixing this requires re-architecture, not just code changes. (2) Data retention and privacy: failure to define data retention policies and privacy requirements (GDPR compliance) can result in legal violations discovered after deployment, requiring expensive redesign of data storage and processing.

rubric.key: Explains why NFRs are commonly overlooked
Explains the cost impact of late discovery

quizzes.topic: IT Quality, Security, and Operations Handoff

questions.question: Explain the concept of 'shift-left' in IT project quality management and describe three specific practices that implement it.
questions.answer: Shift-left means moving quality assurance and security activities earlier in the development lifecycle rather than treating them as late-stage gates. Three practices: (1) Code reviews/pair programming: developers review each other's code during development, catching defects before they enter the test cycle. (2) Static Application Security Testing (SAST): automated security scanning integrated into the CI pipeline that catches vulnerabilities every time code is committed. (3) Test-Driven Development (TDD): writing tests before writing code ensures every feature has automated test coverage from the start, catching regressions immediately.

rubric.key: Explains how each catches issues earlier

questions.question: A software project is going live next week. The operations team says they are not ready to support the system. What should the project manager do?
questions.answer: The PM should: (1) Immediately assess the specific gaps in operational readiness (missing runbooks, untrained staff, no monitoring, missing SLAs), (2) Evaluate whether a delay is possible and what the business impact of delay vs. going live without full support would be, (3) If delay is not possible, implement a risk mitigation plan: extend the hypercare period with project team providing L2/L3 support, prioritize the most critical operational procedures for completion before go-live, (4) Escalate to the sponsor with a clear picture of the risk and options, (5) After go-live, treat operational readiness completion as a parallel workstream with a deadline. Root cause: operational readiness should have been tracked as a project workstream from the start, not discovered as a last-minute gap.

rubric.key: Assesses specific gaps rather than panicking

major_assignments.title: IT Project Management Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive IT project management plan for a medium-complexity IT project including requirements analysis, architecture decision, test strategy, release plan, and operational readiness plan.
objectives: Document functional and non-functional requirements with traceability
Make and justify a build vs. buy/SaaS decision
Design a comprehensive test strategy covering all testing levels
Create a release plan with operational readiness checklist
deliverables: Requirements document with functional, non-functional, and traceability matrix (4-5 pages)
Architecture decision record with build vs. buy analysis (2-3 pages)
Test strategy covering unit through UAT with automation approach (3-4 pages)
Release plan with deployment strategy and rollback procedures (2-3 pages)
Operational readiness checklist and support model design (2-3 pages)

criteria: Functional requirements are clear and testable
Non-functional requirements cover performance, security, and scalability
Traceability matrix links requirements to tests
Build vs. buy analysis is thorough with TCO comparison
Decision is justified with clear rationale
All testing levels covered with clear entry/exit criteria
Rollback plan is specific and tested

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on IT project characteristics, requirements, testing, and operations

it_project_plan.description: Comprehensive IT project management plan assignment

midterm_examination.description: IT project landscape, requirements, and estimation

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all IT PM topics including emerging technology

class_participation.description: Participation in case studies, build vs. buy exercises, and testing workshops

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Hands-on testing workshops require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Case study analyses may be done in groups. The IT PM plan assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Architecture decisions must be supported by evidence, not copied from vendor marketing materials.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to cloud platform free tier (AWS, Azure, or GCP) for hands-on exercises. Familiarity with CI/CD concepts.
